lesson.menu_title: Advanced Negotiation: Pushing for a Better Deal
lesson.menu_desc: Block four begins. Push for a better deal without breaking the relationship: use your leverage, trade a concession for a concession, and know your bottom line. Talk about what could have happened differently with the third conditional (If you had ordered earlier, we would have offered a bigger discount) -- imagining a different past and its result -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 20 -- Advanced Negotiation: Pushing for a Better Deal
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Roberto Rezende | Aula 20 -- Advanced Negotiation: Pushing for a Better Deal

listenings.text: Hi Roberto, this is Amy with a quick brief before your call with the Vinex account. They want a better price, and they are pushing hard. Here is how I want you to handle it. First, remember your leverage. We deliver on time, our quality is high, and they have stayed with us for three years. That is real value, so do not give the price away. Second, never give a concession for nothing. If they want a lower price, ask for something in return, like a bigger volume or a longer contract. A concession for a concession. Third, know your bottom line. The lowest number you can accept is five percent, and that is non-negotiable below that point. If they push past it, you stay calm and you are ready to walk away. Now, one more thing to keep in mind. Last quarter we lost the Orion deal because we waited too long to respond. If we had answered faster, we would have closed it. So this time, move quickly and propose a clear trade-off. You can sweeten the deal with free training, but only if they sign this month. You know this client well. Use your leverage, trade smart, and protect the bottom line. Good luck, Roberto. Talk soon.

This is a short voice note to recap the Vinex negotiation for the file. The client opened by pushing hard on price, so I started from our leverage. I reminded them that we deliver on time, our quality is high, and they have been with us for three years. Then I made it clear: I do not give a concession for nothing. They asked for a lower price, so I asked for a longer contract in return. A concession for a concession. We almost reached a deadlock on the final number, because they wanted more than my bottom line allowed. I stayed calm and I was ready to walk away, but I did not have to. To break the deadlock, I offered a trade-off. I sweetened the deal with free onboarding training, on the condition that they sign this month. Looking back, I learned one thing. If I had prepared the volume numbers earlier, I would have closed even faster. Next time I will have everything ready before the call. In the end, we protected the bottom line, the client signed, and the relationship is stronger than before.

extra_audio.text: First, start from your leverage and remind the client of the value you bring. Then, never give a concession for nothing. Ask for something in return. After that, know your bottom line and the point where the number is non-negotiable. Next, if you reach a deadlock, offer a clear trade-off to move forward. Finally, sweeten the deal only when the client is ready to sign, and protect the relationship.
